draft.introduction: Ambrose’s Letters 17 and 18 (384 CE) are his response to Symmachus’s petition for the Altar of Victory. The Bishop of Milan argued that Christian truth is exclusive and that the empire’s prosperity comes from God, not from pagan sacrifice.

translation.text: If the old religion had been true, it would have availed of old. But now it has been overcome by the truth. Why do you lead me to examples of antiquity? It is shameful to fall back upon the positions of infancy when you have attained the age of reason.
translation.original_text: Si vetus religio vera esset, olim profuisset. Nunc vero veritate superata est. Quid me ad veterum exempla deducis? Pudet in puerorum positionem recidere cum ad rationis aetatem perveneris.

translator_notes.note: Ambrose’s argument frames the pagan-Christian conflict as a matter of maturity: paganism is childhood, Christianity is adulthood. This developmental rhetoric was enormously influential and shaped the Enlightenment’s later (ironic) application of the same logic to Christianity itself.
